questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int x^4\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Power Rule for integration: $\int x^n\,dx = \frac{x^{n+1}}{n+1}+C$. With $n=4$: $\frac{x^5}{5}+C$.
$4x^3$ is the derivative of $x^4$, not its antiderivative. Integration increases the exponent by 1 and divides by the new exponent.
The Power Rule divides by $n+1=5$: the antiderivative is $\frac{x^5}{5}+C$, not $x^5+C$.
This would be the antiderivative of $x^2$, not $x^4$. Add 1 to the exponent: $4+1=5$, giving $\frac{x^5}{5}+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int e^x\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $e^x$ is its own antiderivative: $\frac{d}{dx}[e^x] = e^x$, so $\int e^x\,dx = e^x + C$.
That is NOT the antiderivative. Integration by parts would give $xe^x - e^x$ for $\int xe^x\,dx$. For $\int e^x\,dx$: simply $e^x + C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[e^{x+1}] = e^{x+1}$, not $e^x$. The antiderivative of $e^x$ is $e^x + C$.
The Power Rule for integration does not apply to $e^x$. The antiderivative of $e^x$ is simply $e^x + C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \cos(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\frac{d}{dx}[\sin x] = \cos x$, so $\int \cos x\,dx = \sin x + C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[-\sin x] = -\cos x$. The antiderivative of $\cos x$ is $+\sin x + C$.
Verify: $\frac{d}{dx}[\cos x] = -\sin x \neq \cos x$. The antiderivative of $\cos x$ is $\sin x + C$.
$-\cos x + C$ is the antiderivative of $\sin x$, not $\cos x$. Verify: $\frac{d}{dx}[-\cos x] = \sin x$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{1}{x}\,dx$ for $x > 0$?

choices.explanation: $\frac{d}{dx}[\ln|x|] = \frac{1}{x}$, so $\int \frac{1}{x}\,dx = \ln|x| + C$.
The Power Rule fails for $n=-1$ (division by zero). Instead, $\int x^{-1}\,dx = \ln|x| + C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[\frac{1}{x^2}] = -\frac{2}{x^3}$, not $\frac{1}{x}$. The antiderivative of $\frac{1}{x}$ is $\ln|x|+C$.
$-\frac{1}{x^2}+C$ is the antiderivative of $\frac{2}{x^3}$. For $\frac{1}{x}$: $\int \frac{1}{x}dx = \ln|x|+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^3 (2x+1)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Evaluating $2(3)+1=7$ (the integrand at $x=3$) is not integration. $[x^2+x]_0^3 = 12-0=12$.
$(3)^2+(3) = 12$, not 15. $15$ would result from $[x^2+x]_0^3$ evaluated incorrectly as $[2x^2+x]_0^3/2$.
$[x^2+x]_0^3 = 12$. $6$ would result from integrating $2x$ only ($[x^2]_0^3=9$) or a factor error.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_1^e \frac{1}{x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $[\ln x]_1^e = 1$, not $e$. Note $\ln(e) = 1$, not $e$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: If $F'(x) = f(x)$, which of the following gives $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ by the Fundamental Theorem of Calculus (Part 2)?

choices.explanation: FTC Part 2: $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = F(b)-F(a)$, where $F$ is any antiderivative of $f$.
The FTC uses subtraction, not addition: $\int_a^b f(x)dx = F(b)-F(a)$.
The FTC uses the antiderivative $F$, not the function $f$ itself: $\int_a^b f\,dx = F(b)-F(a)$.
The order matters: $F(b)-F(a)$ (upper limit minus lower limit). Reversing gives the negative of the integral.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \sin(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\frac{d}{dx}[\cos x] = -\sin x$, not $+\sin x$. The antiderivative of $\sin x$ is $-\cos x + C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[-\cos x] = \sin x$, confirming $\int \sin x\,dx = -\cos x + C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[\sin x] = \cos x \neq \sin x$. The antiderivative of $\sin x$ is $-\cos x + C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[-\sin x] = -\cos x$, not $\sin x$. The correct antiderivative is $-\cos x + C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Using a left Riemann sum with 4 equal subintervals, which of the following approximates $\int_0^4 f(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Left Riemann sum with $\Delta x=1$: $f(0)+f(1)+f(2)+f(3) = 2+5+4+7 = 18$.
The right Riemann sum: $f(1)+f(2)+f(3)+f(4) = 5+4+7+3=19$. The left sum uses left endpoints: $f(0)=2$.
Summing all five values $2+5+4+7+3=21$ counts an extra term. Left Riemann sum uses 4 left endpoints only.
$16$ is not the sum of any four consecutive values in this table. Left sum: $f(0)+f(1)+f(2)+f(3) = 2+5+4+7=18$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\frac{d}{dx}\left[\int_0^x \sin(t^2)\,dt\right]$ by FTC Part 1?

choices.explanation: FTC Part 1: differentiate with respect to $x$, not $t$. The result is $\sin(x^2)$ (replace $t$ with $x$).
FTC Part 1: $\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^x f(t)dt = f(x)$. Here $f(t) = \sin(t^2)$, so the derivative is $\sin(x^2)$.
$2x\cos(x^2)$ is the derivative of $\sin(x^2)$, not of the integral $\int_0^x \sin(t^2)dt$. FTC Part 1 simply gives $\sin(x^2)$.
That would be differentiating under the integral sign incorrectly. FTC Part 1 directly gives $\sin(x^2)$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_2^5 f(x)\,dx + \int_5^8 f(x)\,dx$ using properties of definite integrals?

choices.explanation: Additive property: $\int_a^c f = \int_a^b f + \int_b^c f$. So $\int_2^5 f + \int_5^8 f = \int_2^8 f$.
The limits do not add: $5+8\neq 13$. The shared endpoint $x=5$ connects the two integrals: $\int_2^5 + \int_5^8 = \int_2^8$.
This would double the integral. The additive property combines the two into one: $\int_2^8 f$.
$\int_5^5 f = 0$ (zero-width interval). The two integrals combine to $\int_2^8 f$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ when $f(x) \geq 0$ on $[a,b]$?

choices.text: The area between the curve $y = f(x)$ and the $x$-axis on $[a,b]$
choices.explanation: When $f(x) \geq 0$, the definite integral equals the area under the curve and above the $x$-axis on $[a,b]$.

choices.text: The signed area, which could be negative
choices.explanation: Signed area is the general interpretation. When $f(x) \geq 0$, the signed area equals the actual (unsigned) area, which is non-negative.
$f(b)-f(a)$ is the net change in $f$. The definite integral is the area under $f$ on $[a,b]$ (when $f\geq 0$).
The FTC gives $\int_a^b f = F(b)-F(a)$, not $F(a)-F(b)$. Order matters.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\pi} \sin(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\int_0^\pi \sin x\,dx = [-\cos x]_0^\pi = -\cos(\pi)+\cos(0) = 1+1=2$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^2 3x^2\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $3x^2\big|_{x=2} = 12$ is the integrand value at $x=2$, not the integral. $[x^3]_0^2 = 8$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Using a right Riemann sum with 4 equal subintervals, which of the following approximates $\int_0^4 f(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Right Riemann sum with $\Delta x=1$: $f(1)+f(2)+f(3)+f(4) = 4+3+6+2=15$.
Midpoint sum or trapezoidal approximation might give 16. Right sum uses right endpoints: $4+3+6+2=15$.
$9$ sums only $f(3)+f(4)$. The right sum uses all four right endpoints: $f(1)+f(2)+f(3)+f(4)=15$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int x\cos(x^2)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Let $u = x^2$, $du = 2x\,dx$. $\int x\cos(x^2)dx = \frac{1}{2}\int \cos(u)du = \frac{1}{2}\sin(u)+C = \frac{1}{2}\sin(x^2)+C$.
Missing the factor $\frac{1}{2}$. $u$-sub gives $\frac{1}{2}\int\cos u\,du = \frac{1}{2}\sin(x^2)+C$.
$-\frac{1}{2}\cos(x^2)$ is the antiderivative of $x\sin(x^2)$. For $x\cos(x^2)$: the result is $+\frac{1}{2}\sin(x^2)+C$.
Verify: $\frac{d}{dx}[x\sin(x^2)] = \sin(x^2)+2x^2\cos(x^2)\neq x\cos(x^2)$. Use $u$-substitution: $\frac{1}{2}\sin(x^2)+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^1 2xe^{x^2}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $[e^{x^2}]_0^1 = e^1-e^0 = e-1$, not $e$. Subtract the lower-bound value $e^0=1$.
The $2x$ factor is the exact differential of $x^2$, so no extra factor of 2 remains after $u$-sub. $e^u$ from 0 to 1 gives $e-1$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\frac{d}{dx}\left[\int_0^{x^2} e^t\,dt\right]$?

choices.explanation: FTC Part 1 with chain rule: $\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{x^2}e^t\,dt = e^{x^2} \cdot \frac{d}{dx}[x^2] = 2xe^{x^2}$.
FTC Part 1 + chain rule: $\frac{d}{dx}\int_0^{g(x)}f(t)dt = f(g(x)) \cdot g'(x)$. Here $f(t)=e^t$, $g(x)=x^2$: $e^{x^2} \cdot 2x$.
$e^{x^2}-1$ would be the value of the integral evaluated ($e^{x^2}-e^0$), not its derivative. Apply FTC Part 1 with chain rule: $2xe^{x^2}$.
The chain rule factor is $g'(x) = 2x$, not $x^2$. FTC Part 1 gives $e^{x^2} \cdot 2x = 2xe^{x^2}$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{3x^2}{x^3+1}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $u = x^3+1$, $du=3x^2\,dx$. $\int\frac{3x^2}{x^3+1}dx = \int\frac{du}{u} = \ln|u|+C = \ln|x^3+1|+C$.
This equals 1, which is constant. $u$-substitution gives $\ln|x^3+1|+C$.
Division by $3x^2$ is incorrect. The $u$-sub $u=x^3+1$ absorbs the $3x^2$ in the numerator entirely.
After $u$-sub, no factor of 3 remains: $\int \frac{du}{u} = \ln|u|+C = \ln|x^3+1|+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Using the trapezoidal rule with 4 equal subintervals, which of the following approximates $\int_0^4 f(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Summing all 5 values: $4+6+5+8+3=26$ is not the trapezoidal rule. The trap rule halves the first and last terms.
The correct trapezoidal sum is $\frac{1}{2}(4+12+10+16+3) = 22.5$, not 22.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int (x+1)^{10}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $10(x+1)^9$ is the derivative of $(x+1)^{10}$, not its antiderivative. Integration increases the exponent.
The exponent after integration is $10+1=11$: $\frac{(x+1)^{11}}{11}+C$.
The coefficient should be $\frac{1}{11}$, not $11$. $u$-sub gives $\frac{u^{11}}{11}+C = \frac{(x+1)^{11}}{11}+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: If $f$ is an odd function and $\int_0^3 f(x)\,dx = 5$, which of the following gives $\int_{-3}^3 f(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: For an even function: $\int_{-3}^3 f = 2\int_0^3 f = 10$. For an ODD function: $\int_{-3}^3 f = 0$.
For odd functions, $f(-x) = -f(x)$: $\int_{-3}^3 f(x)dx = \int_{-3}^0 f + \int_0^3 f = -5+5 = 0$.
That is $\int_0^3 f$, not $\int_{-3}^3 f$. For an odd function, $\int_{-a}^a f = 0$.
$\int_{-3}^0 f = -5$ for an odd function. But $\int_{-3}^3 f = \int_{-3}^0 f + \int_0^3 f = -5+5=0$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_1^4 \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\int_1^4 \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}dx = [\sqrt{x}]_1^4 = 2-1=1$. Note $\frac{d}{dx}[\sqrt{x}] = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$.
The antiderivative of $\frac{1}{2\sqrt{x}}$ is $\sqrt{x}$. $[\sqrt{x}]_1^4 = 2-1=1$, not $\frac{1}{2}$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\frac{d}{dx}\left[\int_{x}^{5} t^3\,dt\right]$?

choices.explanation: The lower limit is $x$, not the upper. Reverse the limits: $\int_x^5 t^3 dt = -\int_5^x t^3 dt$. Derivative: $-x^3$.
FTC Part 1: $\frac{d}{dx}\int_x^5 t^3 dt = -\frac{d}{dx}\int_5^x t^3 dt = -x^3$.
$5^3=125$ is the constant upper-limit value of the integrand. FTC Part 1 differentiates with respect to the variable limit.
That is not a result of FTC differentiation. The derivative of $\int_x^5 t^3 dt$ with respect to $x$ is $-x^3$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \sec^2(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\frac{d}{dx}[\tan x] = \sec^2 x$, so $\int \sec^2 x\,dx = \tan x + C$.
$\sec x\tan x$ is the derivative of $\sec x$, not $\sec^2 x$. The antiderivative of $\sec^2 x$ is $\tan x + C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[\sec^2 x] = 2\sec x \sec x\tan x$, not the antiderivative. $\int\sec^2 x\,dx = \tan x+C$.
$-\cot x + C$ is the antiderivative of $\csc^2 x$. For $\sec^2 x$: antiderivative is $\tan x + C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos(2x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\frac{\sin(\pi)}{2} - \frac{\sin(0)}{2} = 0-0=0$. The result is 0.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^4 |x-2|\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $|x-2|$ is never negative: $\int_0^4|x-2|dx>0$. Split at $x=2$: $\int_0^2(2-x)dx + \int_2^4(x-2)dx = 2+2=4$.
Each half contributes 2; the total is $2+2=4$.
Each triangular region has area 2; total is 4, not 8.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{x}{x^2+4}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $u=x^2+4$, $du=2x\,dx$. $\int\frac{x}{x^2+4}dx = \frac{1}{2}\int\frac{du}{u} = \frac{1}{2}\ln|u|+C = \frac{1}{2}\ln|x^2+4|+C$.
Missing the factor $\frac{1}{2}$ from $u$-sub. $du=2x\,dx$ introduces $\frac{1}{2}$: $\frac{1}{2}\ln|x^2+4|+C$.
That is $\int\frac{1}{x^2+4}dx$, not $\int\frac{x}{x^2+4}dx$. With $x$ in the numerator, use $u$-sub: $\frac{1}{2}\ln|x^2+4|+C$.
That is not an antiderivative. $u$-substitution correctly gives $\frac{1}{2}\ln|x^2+4|+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

stimulus.content: A particle moves with velocity $v(t) = 3t^2 - 6t$ for $0 \leq t \leq 3$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives the displacement of the particle on $[0,3]$?

choices.explanation: $[t^3-3t^2]_0^3 = 0$. $27$ is $t^3$ evaluated at $t=3$ without subtracting $3t^2$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_1^2 \frac{\ln(x)}{x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\ln 2 = \int_1^2 \frac{1}{x}dx$, not $\int_1^2\frac{\ln x}{x}dx$. The latter has $u=\ln x$ and gives $\frac{(\ln 2)^2}{2}$.
The $u$-sub gives $\frac{(\ln 2)^2}{2}$ (the square of $\ln 2$, divided by 2), not $\frac{\ln 2}{2}$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: If $\int_0^5 f(x)\,dx = 10$ and $\int_3^5 f(x)\,dx = 4$, which of the following gives $\int_0^3 f(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Adding instead of subtracting: $10+4=14$. But $\int_0^3 f = \int_0^5 f - \int_3^5 f = 6$.
No multiplication is involved. $\int_0^3 f = \int_0^5 f - \int_3^5 f = 10-4=6$.
$4 = \int_3^5 f$, not $\int_0^3 f$. The latter is $10-4=6$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{e^x}{e^x+1}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $u=e^x+1$, $du=e^x\,dx$. $\int\frac{e^x}{e^x+1}dx = \int\frac{du}{u} = \ln|u|+C = \ln|e^x+1|+C$.
That is the integrand, not its antiderivative. $u$-sub gives $\ln|e^x+1|+C$.
This is not a valid antiderivative. $u$-substitution correctly gives $\ln(e^x+1)+C$.
This is not a standard antiderivative form. $u=e^x+1$ gives $\int du/u = \ln|u|+C = \ln(e^x+1)+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

stimulus.content: The velocity of a particle is $v(t) = 6 - 2t$ ft/s for $0 \leq t \leq 5$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives the total distance traveled by the particle on $[0, 5]$?

choices.explanation: Net displacement $= \int_0^5(6-2t)dt = [6t-t^2]_0^5 = 30-25=5$. Total distance requires integrating $|v|$.
Total distance $\neq$ twice the net displacement. Calculate $|v|$ piecewise: $9+4=13$ ft.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \tan(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\ln|\cos x| + C$ differentiates to $\frac{-\sin x}{\cos x} = -\tan x$, not $+\tan x$.
$\int\tan x\,dx = \int\frac{\sin x}{\cos x}dx$. Let $u=\cos x$, $du=-\sin x\,dx$: $-\int\frac{du}{u} = -\ln|u|+C = -\ln|\cos x|+C$.
$\sec^2 x$ is the derivative of $\tan x$, not its antiderivative. $\int\tan x\,dx = -\ln|\cos x|+C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[\ln|\sin x|] = \frac{\cos x}{\sin x} = \cot x$, not $\tan x$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^1 \frac{x}{\sqrt{1-x^2}}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: The integrand is positive on $(0,1)$, so the integral is positive. $[\sqrt{1-x^2}]$ from 0 to 1: $\sqrt{u}$ evaluated at 0 and 1 gives $1-0=1$.
While the integrand has a singularity at $x=1$, the integral converges (improper integral equals 1 via $u$-sub).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{x^2+1}{x^3+3x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: The $u$-sub introduces $\frac{1}{3}$: $du = 3(x^2+1)dx$, so $x^2+1 = \frac{du}{3}$. The answer is $\frac{1}{3}\ln|x^3+3x|+C$.
This is not a standard form. $u$-substitution cleanly gives $\frac{1}{3}\ln|x^3+3x|+C$.
The constant factor from $u$-sub is $\frac{1}{3}$ (the reciprocal of the derivative coefficient), not $\frac{1}{3x^2+3}$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\pi/4} \tan^2(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\int_0^{\pi/4}\tan^2 x = 1 - \pi/4 \approx 0.215$. Using $\pi/4$ ignores the identity $\tan^2 x = \sec^2 x-1$.
$[\tan x - x]_0^{\pi/4} = 1-\pi/4 \neq 1$. The $-x$ term contributes $-\pi/4$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int x\sqrt{x+1}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: This is not correct. $u$-sub gives $\frac{2(x+1)^{5/2}}{5} - \frac{2(x+1)^{3/2}}{3}+C$, which has two terms.
This is the antiderivative of $\sqrt{x+1}$, not $x\sqrt{x+1}$. The $x$ factor introduces an additional term.
Not a valid antiderivative. Use $u=x+1$ to get $\frac{2(x+1)^{5/2}}{5}-\frac{2(x+1)^{3/2}}{3}+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

stimulus.content: A continuous function $f$ satisfies $f(0) = 2$ and $\int_0^x f(t)\,dt = e^x - x - 1 + 2x$ for all $x$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives $f(x)$?

choices.explanation: Differentiate: $f(x) = \frac{d}{dx}[e^x - x - 1 + 2x] = e^x - 1 + 2 = e^x + 1$. Check: $f(0) = 1+1=2$ ✓. So $f(x) = e^x+1$.
By FTC Part 1: $f(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\int_0^x f(t)dt = \frac{d}{dx}[e^x-x-1+2x] = e^x-1+2 = e^x+1$. Verify: $f(0)=2$ ✓.
Differentiating $e^x-x-1+2x = e^x+x-1$ gives $e^x+1$, not $e^x$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{1}{x^2-4}\,dx$ using partial fractions?

choices.explanation: Partial fractions: $\frac{1}{x^2-4} = \frac{1}{(x-2)(x+2)} = \frac{1/4}{x-2} - \frac{1/4}{x+2}$. Integrate: $\frac{1}{4}\ln|x-2| - \frac{1}{4}\ln|x+2| = \frac{1}{4}\ln\left|\frac{x-2}{x+2}\right|+C$.
This is not a valid antiderivative. Partial fractions decompose the integrand before integrating.
$\frac{d}{dx}[\ln|x^2-4|] = \frac{2x}{x^2-4} \neq \frac{1}{x^2-4}$. Partial fractions give $\frac{1}{4}\ln|\frac{x-2}{x+2}|+C$.
Sign error. $\frac{1}{4}\ln|x-2| - \frac{1}{4}\ln|x+2| = \frac{1}{4}\ln\left|\frac{x-2}{x+2}\right|$ (positive $\frac{1}{4}$).

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\frac{d}{dx}\left[\int_{\sin x}^{\cos x} t^2\,dt\right]$?

choices.explanation: Chain rule on both limits: $\frac{d}{dx}\int_a^{g(x)} f = f(g(x))g'(x) - f(h(x))h'(x)$. Here: $\cos^2(x)\cdot(-\sin x) - \sin^2(x)\cdot\cos(x)$.
This omits the chain rule factors $g'(x) = -\sin x$ and $h'(x) = \cos x$. Apply Leibniz rule.
This is $F(\cos x) - F(\sin x)$ (the value of the integral), not its derivative.
While $\cos^2+\sin^2=1$, this does not follow from differentiating the integral. Apply Leibniz rule with chain factors.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \sin^2(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Use $\sin^2 x = \frac{1-\cos(2x)}{2}$: $\int\frac{1-\cos 2x}{2}dx = \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4}+C$.
Sign error. $\int\frac{1-\cos 2x}{2}dx = \frac{x}{2} - \frac{\sin 2x}{4}+C$. The $\cos(2x)$ term integrates with a minus sign.
Note $-\sin x\cos x = -\frac{\sin 2x}{2}$. $\int\sin^2 x\,dx = \frac{x}{2}-\frac{\sin 2x}{4}+C$, which has an $x$ term.
$\frac{d}{dx}[\sin^3 x/3] = \sin^2 x \cos x \neq \sin^2 x$. The half-angle identity is needed.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Using a midpoint Riemann sum with 2 equal subintervals to approximate $\int_0^2 f'(x)\,dx$, which of the following is closest?

choices.explanation: Midpoint sum with 2 subintervals ($[0,1]$ and $[1,2]$), $\Delta x=1$: midpoints at $x=0.5$ and $x=1.5$. Sum $= 1 \cdot (f'(0.5)+f'(1.5)) = 2+1=3$, not 4.
Using $f'(1)+f'(1) = 4+4=8$ or other endpoints. Midpoints of $[0,1]$ and $[1,2]$ are $0.5$ and $1.5$.
Midpoint rule, 2 subintervals $[0,1]$ and $[1,2]$, $\Delta x=1$. Midpoints: $x=0.5$ and $x=1.5$. Sum $= 1\cdot(f'(0.5)+f'(1.5)) = 2+1=3$.
Using $f'(1)+f'(2)/2$ or other combinations. Midpoint rule at $x=0.5,1.5$: $2+1=3$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: If $f$ is decreasing on $[a,b]$, which of the following correctly describes the relationship between the left and right Riemann sums and the actual integral?

choices.text: Right Riemann sum $\geq \int_a^b f(x)\,dx \geq$ Left Riemann sum
choices.explanation: For a decreasing function, left endpoints give larger values (overestimate) and right endpoints give smaller values (underestimate). The correct ordering is left sum $\geq$ integral $\geq$ right sum, not the reverse.
For decreasing $f$: left endpoints give higher values (overestimate); right endpoints give lower values (underestimate). Left $\geq$ integral $\geq$ right.

choices.text: Left sum overestimates, right sum underestimates
choices.explanation: For a decreasing function, left endpoints have larger $f$ values (overestimate), and right endpoints have smaller $f$ values (underestimate).

choices.text: Left sum underestimates, right sum overestimates
choices.explanation: This is the relationship for INCREASING functions. For decreasing functions, left sums overestimate and right sums underestimate.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{1}{\sqrt{4-x^2}}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\frac{1}{\sqrt{4-x^2}} = \frac{1}{2\sqrt{1-(x/2)^2}}$. Using $\int\frac{1}{\sqrt{a^2-x^2}}dx = \arcsin(x/a)+C$ with $a=2$: $\arcsin(x/2)+C$.
The standard formula $\int\frac{dx}{\sqrt{a^2-x^2}} = \arcsin(x/a)+C$ (no extra $1/a$ factor). Result: $\arcsin(x/2)+C$.
$\arctan(x/2)$ antiderivative requires $\frac{1}{x^2+4}$ form, not $\frac{1}{\sqrt{4-x^2}}$. The answer is $\arcsin(x/2)+C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[-\sqrt{4-x^2}] = \frac{x}{\sqrt{4-x^2}} \neq \frac{1}{\sqrt{4-x^2}}$. The correct antiderivative is $\arcsin(x/2)+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

stimulus.content: A function $g$ is defined by $g(x) = \int_0^x f(t)\,dt$ where $f$ has the following properties: $f(t) > 0$ on $(0,3)$, $f(t) < 0$ on $(3,6)$, and $\int_0^3 f = 5$, $\int_3^6 f = -3$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives the absolute maximum of $g$ on $[0,6]$?

choices.explanation: $g(3) = \int_0^3 f = 5$. For $x>3$: $g$ decreases (since $f<0$). For $x<3$: $g$ increases. Maximum is $g(3)=5$.
$g(0)=0 < g(3)=5$. The function increases on $(0,3)$, reaching a maximum at $x=3$.

choices.text: Cannot be determined from the given information
choices.explanation: We can determine: $g$ increases on $(0,3)$ (since $f>0$) and decreases on $(3,6)$ (since $f<0$). Maximum at $x=3$: $g(3)=5$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\pi/2} \sin(x)\cos(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Use $\sin x \cos x = \frac{\sin 2x}{2}$: $\int_0^{\pi/2}\frac{\sin 2x}{2}dx = [-\frac{\cos 2x}{4}]_0^{\pi/2} = (-\frac{\cos\pi}{4})+\frac{\cos 0}{4} = \frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4} = \frac{1}{2}$.
$\frac{1}{4}$ is each individual piece ($\frac{1}{4}$), but both limits contribute: $\frac{1}{4}+\frac{1}{4}=\frac{1}{2}$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int e^{\sqrt{x}}\,dx$? (Hint: use $u=\sqrt{x}$)

choices.explanation: $u=\sqrt{x}$, $x=u^2$, $dx=2u\,du$. $\int e^u \cdot 2u\,du = 2\int ue^u\,du = 2(ue^u-e^u)+C = 2e^u(u-1)+C = 2e^{\sqrt{x}}(\sqrt{x}-1)+C$.
Integration by parts gives $2ue^u - 2e^u = 2e^u(u-1)$, not just $2ue^u$. The $-2e^u$ term is missing.
$\frac{d}{dx}\left[\frac{e^{\sqrt{x}}}{\sqrt{x}}\right]$ is not $e^{\sqrt{x}}$. The correct antiderivative is $2(\sqrt{x}-1)e^{\sqrt{x}}+C$.
$2(u-1)e^u = 2ue^u-2e^u = 2e^u(u-1)$, which means the factor is $(\sqrt{x}-1)$, not $(\sqrt{x}+1)$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following correctly identifies whether the trapezoidal approximation overestimates or underestimates $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx$ when $f$ is concave up on $[a,b]$?

choices.text: Underestimates, because the trapezoids lie below the curve
choices.explanation: For concave up curves, the curve lies BELOW the line connecting endpoints. Trapezoids (using secant lines) lie ABOVE the curve, giving an overestimate.

choices.text: Overestimates, because the trapezoids lie above the curve
choices.explanation: Concave up: the curve bends downward relative to any secant line. Trapezoids use the secant (chord), which lies above the curve, giving an overestimate.

choices.text: Exact, because the trapezoid is tangent to the curve
choices.explanation: Tangent lines give the midpoint approximation (which underestimates for concave up). Trapezoids (secant lines) overestimate.

choices.text: The relationship depends on whether $f'$ is increasing or decreasing
choices.explanation: $f$ concave up means $f'' > 0$ (equivalently, $f'$ is increasing). For concave up functions, trapezoidal approximations always overestimate.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_1^e x^2 \ln(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Forgetting the $-\frac{x^3}{9}$ term and the lower-bound contribution. Full IBP gives $\frac{2e^3+1}{9}$.
The lower-bound contribution: $-\frac{1^3}{9}(\ln 1 - 1/3) = -\frac{1}{9}(0-1/3) = +\frac{1}{9}$. So the answer has $+1$, not $-1$: $\frac{2e^3+1}{9}$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{2x+1}{x^2+x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Note $\frac{d}{dx}[x^2+x] = 2x+1$. So $\int\frac{2x+1}{x^2+x}dx = \ln|x^2+x|+C$ (direct $u$-sub with $u=x^2+x$).
No extra $\frac{1}{2}$ needed: $du = (2x+1)dx$ exactly, so $\int\frac{du}{u} = \ln|u|+C$ with no scaling.
The denominator is $x^2+x$, not $2x+1$. $u=x^2+x$ gives $\ln|x^2+x|+C$.
$du = (2x+1)dx$ exactly matches the numerator with no extra coefficient. The answer is $\ln|x^2+x|+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^2 \sqrt{4-x^2}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $\int_0^2\sqrt{4-x^2}\,dx$ is the area of a quarter circle of radius 2: $\frac{1}{4}\pi(2)^2 = \pi$.
The full circle has area $\pi r^2 = 4\pi$. The quarter circle is $\frac{4\pi}{4} = \pi$.
Quarter circle of radius 2: $\frac{1}{4}\pi(4) = \pi$, not $\pi/2$.
The integral gives the area of a quarter circle (a geometric region), not a rectangle. Area $= \pi$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^1 \frac{x^3}{\sqrt{1+x^4}}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Off by a factor of 4. The $u$-sub introduces $\frac{1}{4}$: $\frac{1}{4}\cdot[2\sqrt{u}]_1^2 = \frac{1}{2}(\sqrt{2}-1)$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{x^3+x}{x^2+1}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Long division: $\frac{x^3+x}{x^2+1} = \frac{x(x^2+1)}{x^2+1} = x$. $\int x\,dx = \frac{x^2}{2}+C$.
$\frac{x^3+x}{x^2+1} = x$ exactly (after factoring). No remainder means no $\arctan$ term.
This is the integrand multiplied by $x/2$, not the antiderivative. Simplify first: $x^3+x = x(x^2+1)$, so the integrand reduces to $x$.
Not a valid form here. The integrand simplifies to $x$, whose antiderivative is $\frac{x^2}{2}+C$.

stimulus.content: To integrate $\int \frac{x^3+x}{x^2+1}\,dx$, perform polynomial long division: $\frac{x^3+x}{x^2+1} = x$. Therefore $\int \frac{x^3+x}{x^2+1}\,dx = \int x\,dx = \frac{x^2}{2}+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

stimulus.content: An accumulation function is defined by $G(x) = \int_2^x f(t)\,dt$ where $f$ is continuous. It is known that $G(2)=0$, $G(5)=7$, and $f(5)=3$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives $G'(5)$?

choices.explanation: $G(5)=7$ is the accumulated area, not $G'(5)$. By FTC Part 1: $G'(x) = f(x)$, so $G'(5) = f(5) = 3$.
FTC Part 1: $G'(x) = \frac{d}{dx}\int_2^x f(t)dt = f(x)$. Therefore $G'(5) = f(5) = 3$.
$G'(5)=f(5)=3 \neq 0$. $G'=0$ only when $f=0$ at that point.
$\frac{G(5)}{f(5)} = \frac{7}{3}$ has no standard calculus meaning. By FTC Part 1: $G'(5)=f(5)=3$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\pi} f(x)\,dx$ if $\int_0^{\pi} f(x)\,dx = \int_0^{\pi/2} f(x)\,dx + 4$ and $\int_0^{\pi/2} f(x)\,dx = 3$?

choices.explanation: $\int_0^\pi f = \int_0^{\pi/2}f + \int_{\pi/2}^\pi f$. We also know $\int_0^\pi f = \int_0^{\pi/2}f + 4$, so $\int_0^\pi f = 3 + 4 = 7$.
$4 = \int_{\pi/2}^\pi f$ (the second piece). The full integral $\int_0^\pi f = 3+4=7$.
Subtracting instead of adding: $3-4=-1$. But the given relation adds 4: $\int_0^\pi f = 3+4=7$.

stimulus.content: Given: $\int_0^{\pi} f(x)\,dx = \int_0^{\pi/2} f(x)\,dx + 4$ and $\int_0^{\pi/2} f(x)\,dx = ?$. Use the additive property: $\int_0^{\pi} f(x)\,dx = \int_0^{\pi/2} f(x)\,dx + \int_{\pi/2}^{\pi} f(x)\,dx$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\ln 2} e^x\sqrt{e^x+1}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $u=e^x+1$, $du=e^x dx$. Limits: $x=0\Rightarrow u=2$; $x=\ln 2\Rightarrow u=3$. $\int_2^3\sqrt{u}\,du = [\frac{2u^{3/2}}{3}]_2^3 = \frac{2}{3}(3^{3/2}-2^{3/2})$.
Limits after $u$-sub: lower is $u=2$, upper is $u=3$. Correct order: $[\frac{2u^{3/2}}{3}]_2^3 = \frac{2}{3}(3^{3/2}-2^{3/2})$.
$3^{3/2}=3\sqrt{3}$ and $2^{3/2}=2\sqrt{2}$. The expression $\frac{2}{3}(3^{3/2}-2^{3/2})$ is the correct form.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following correctly evaluates $\int_{-1}^{1} x^5 e^{x^2}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $f(x) = x^5 e^{x^2}$ is an odd function: $f(-x) = (-x)^5 e^{(-x)^2} = -x^5 e^{x^2} = -f(x)$. For odd functions: $\int_{-1}^1 f = 0$.
$x^5 e^{x^2}$ is odd (product of odd $x^5$ and even $e^{x^2}$). For odd functions symmetric about origin: $\int_{-a}^a f = 0$.
That would be for even functions. Since $x^5 e^{x^2}$ is odd, $\int_{-1}^1 = 0$.
Recognizing the odd symmetry eliminates computation: $\int_{-1}^1 x^5 e^{x^2}dx = 0$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin^2 x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $u=\sin x$, $du=\cos x\,dx$. $\int\frac{\cos x}{1+\sin^2 x}dx = \int\frac{du}{1+u^2} = \arctan(u)+C = \arctan(\sin x)+C$.
After $u$-sub: $\int\frac{du}{1+u^2} = \arctan(u)+C$, not $\arcsin$. The denominator $1+u^2$ gives $\arctan$.
That is the integrand, not its antiderivative. $u$-sub gives $\arctan(\sin x)+C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[\ln(1+\sin^2 x)] = \frac{2\sin x\cos x}{1+\sin^2 x} \neq \frac{\cos x}{1+\sin^2 x}$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

stimulus.content: A particle starts at position $s = 5$ and has velocity $v(t) = \sin(\pi t)$ for $t \geq 0$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives the position at $t = 2$?

choices.explanation: $\int_0^2\sin(\pi t)dt = \frac{-\cos 2\pi + \cos 0}{\pi} = \frac{-1+1}{\pi} = 0$. So $s(2) = 5+0=5$.
The net displacement $\int_0^2\sin(\pi t)dt = 0$ (complete oscillation). Position returns to $s=5$.
$\int_0^2\sin(\pi t)dt = \frac{-\cos(2\pi)+1}{\pi} = 0$. Position is $5+0=5$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{x^2-x+1}{x^3-x}\,dx$? (Hint: factor $x^3-x = x(x-1)(x+1)$)

choices.explanation: This uses $C = 1/2$, but the correct value is $C = 3/2$. At $x = -1$: $C(-1)(-2) = 1 + 1 + 1 = 3$, so $2C = 3$ and $C = 3/2$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[\ln|x^3-x|] = \frac{3x^2-1}{x^3-x}$, not $\frac{x^2-x+1}{x^3-x}$. Partial fractions are needed.
This would be the antiderivative if the numerator were exactly $\frac{3x^2-1}{3}$, which it is not. Partial fractions give separate $\ln$ terms.

stimulus.content: To integrate $\int \frac{x^2-x+1}{x^3-x}\,dx$, factor: $x^3-x = x(x-1)(x+1)$. Use partial fractions: $\frac{x^2-x+1}{x(x-1)(x+1)} = \frac{A}{x}+\frac{B}{x-1}+\frac{C}{x+1}$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^{\pi/2} \cos^3(x)\,dx$?

choices.explanation: Use $\cos^3 x = \cos x(1-\sin^2 x)$ and $u$-sub to get $2/3$, not $\pi/4$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int \frac{1}{x\ln x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $u=\ln x$, $du=\frac{1}{x}dx$. $\int\frac{1}{x\ln x}dx = \int\frac{du}{u} = \ln|u|+C = \ln|\ln x|+C$.
Not a valid antiderivative. $u$-sub gives $\int du/u = \ln|u| = \ln|\ln x|+C$.
That is $\int \ln x \cdot \frac{1}{x}dx$, not $\int\frac{1}{x\ln x}dx$. Here the numerator is 1, giving $\ln|\ln x|+C$.
$\frac{d}{dx}[-1/\ln x] = \frac{1}{x(\ln x)^2} \neq \frac{1}{x\ln x}$. The correct antiderivative is $\ln|\ln x|+C$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

stimulus.content: A function $F$ is an antiderivative of $f$, and $\int_a^b f(x)\,dx = 12$. Also, $F(a) = 3$.

questions.question: Which of the following gives $F(b)$?

choices.explanation: FTC Part 2: $\int_a^b f = F(b)-F(a) \Rightarrow 12 = F(b)-3 \Rightarrow F(b) = 15$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_1^e \frac{(\ln x)^2}{x}\,dx$?

choices.explanation: $u=\ln x$, $du=\frac{1}{x}dx$. Limits: $x=1\Rightarrow u=0$; $x=e\Rightarrow u=1$. $\int_0^1 u^2 du = [u^3/3]_0^1 = 1/3$.
$[u^3/3]_0^1 = 1/3$, not $1/2$. The exponent is 2 (not 1), giving $u^3/3$ after integration.
$e-1$ is $\int_1^e\frac{1}{x}dx$, not $\int_1^e\frac{(\ln x)^2}{x}dx$.

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab

questions.question: Which of the following gives $\int_0^4 f(x)\,dx$ if $f(x) = \begin{cases} x & 0 \leq x < 2 \\ 4-x & 2 \leq x \leq 4 \end{cases}$?

choices.explanation: Each piece gives area 2; combined: $2+2=4$.

datasets.label: f(x): x for x<2, 4-x for x>=2

questions.subject: ap-calculus-ab
